luke_galilee.title: The Birth Narratives -- Magnificat, Benedictus, and the Light to the Nations
luke_galilee.synopsis: Luke's birth narratives are the most elaborate in the NT, framing Jesus' arrival in the language and cadence of the Old Testament. The angel Gabriel appears to Zechariah in the Temple to announce the birth of John the Baptist (1:5-25), then to Mary in Nazareth to announce the conception of Jesus: 'The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High (dynamis hypsistou) will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy -- the Son of God (huios theou)' (1:35). Mary's Magnificat (1:46-55) celebrates divine reversal: the mighty pulled down, the humble exalted, the hungry filled, the rich sent away empty. Zechariah's Benedictus (1:68-79) proclaims the Davidic covenant fulfilled. Jesus is born in Bethlehem, placed in a manger. Angels announce to shepherds: 'For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Savior (soter), who is Christ the Lord (Christos Kyrios)' (2:11). The heavenly host praises: 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace among those with whom he is pleased!' (2:14). At the Temple, Simeon prophesies: 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to your people Israel' (2:32). Anna the prophetess confirms the redemption of Jerusalem (2:38). At age 12, Jesus is found in the Temple: 'Did you not know that I must be in my Father's house?' (2:49).

key_verse.text: And the angel answered her, 'The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy -- the Son of God.'

original_terms: hypsistos (Most High -- Greek translation of Hebrew elyon; the divine council title for the supreme God (Deut 32:8 LXX); Gabriel uses it for the Father)
Christos Kyrios (Christ the Lord / Messiah the Lord -- the double title announced to the shepherds; combines the messianic office with the divine name)
soter (Savior -- the angelic announcement's title for Jesus; used for YHWH in the OT: 'I, I am the LORD, and besides me there is no savior,' Isa 43:11)
phos eis apokalypsin ethnon (a light for revelation to the Gentiles -- Simeon's quotation of Isa 42:6/49:6; the universal scope of Jesus' mission)
megalynei (magnifies -- 'My soul magnifies (megalynei) the Lord'; the opening verb of the Magnificat; Mary declares YHWH's greatness)
diatheke (covenant -- 'the oath that he swore to our father Abraham' (1:73); the Abrahamic covenant fulfilled in the birth of the Messiah)
luke_galilee.ane_backdrop: The birth narratives draw on the ANE motif of the divine birth announcement -- an angelic messenger appears to announce the birth of a divinely chosen child. Parallels include the birth of Isaac (Gen 18:10-14), Samson (Judg 13:3-5), and Samuel (1 Sam 1:17-20). The Magnificat's theme of divine reversal -- the mighty brought low, the humble exalted -- echoes Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:1-10) and the broader ANE wisdom tradition about the gods overturning human social orders. The angelic host's appearance to the shepherds (2:8-14) is a theophanic event: the 'glory of the Lord' (doxa kyriou) shining around them evokes the shekinah glory of the Temple and the Sinai theophany. The census under Augustus (2:1-2) serves as an ironic counterpoint: the Roman emperor thinks he rules the world, but his census merely fulfills the prophetic plan that the Messiah be born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2).

second_temple.summary: This Qumran fragment describes a figure called 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High' who will arise and whose kingdom will be eternal. 'He shall be hailed as Son of God, and they shall call him Son of the Most High.'
second_temple.relevance: The exact titles used in Gabriel's annunciation to Mary (Luke 1:32, 35) -- 'Son of the Most High' and 'Son of God' -- appear in this pre-Christian Jewish text, confirming these were recognized messianic/divine titles.

second_temple.summary: The expected 'Son of David' will 'purge Jerusalem from Gentiles... destroy the unrighteous rulers... and shepherd the Lord's flock in faithfulness.'
second_temple.relevance: Provides the context for the Davidic expectations that Gabriel's announcement fulfills -- though Jesus' kingship will be very different from the military messiah of the Psalms of Solomon.

cross_refs.note: Hannah's prayer -- the literary model for the Magnificat; divine reversal of the proud and humble
'A light to the nations' -- Simeon quotes the Servant Songs, identifying Jesus as the Servant who illuminates the Gentiles
'But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah... from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel' -- fulfilled by the census and birth in Bethlehem
The Davidic covenant: 'I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever' -- echoed in Gabriel's words: 'the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David' (1:32)
'His dominion is an everlasting dominion... his kingdom shall not be destroyed' -- echoed in 'of his kingdom there will be no end' (1:33)
Matthew's parallel birth narrative -- different details (genealogy, Magi, flight to Egypt) but the same theological claims

luke_galilee.divine_council_note: The birth narratives are dense with divine council language and action. Gabriel -- one of only two named angels in the Protestant OT (the other is Michael; both appear in Daniel) -- serves as the divine council messenger who delivers the Father's decrees. His annunciation to Mary uses the divine council title 'Most High' (hypsistos = Hebrew elyon): 'He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High (huios hypsistou). And the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever, and of his kingdom there will be no end' (1:32-33). 'Son of the Most High' is a divine council designation -- the same language used in Psalm 82:6 ('sons of the Most High') for the elohim members of the council. But Jesus is not merely one of the bene elyon -- he is THE Son, unique, with an eternal kingdom. The angelic host (stratia ouraniou, 'heavenly army,' 2:13) that appears to the shepherds is the divine council's military wing -- the same 'host of heaven' that 1 Kings 22:19 describes standing around YHWH's throne. Their proclamation of 'peace on earth' (2:14) is a divine council decree: the King has arrived, and his reign brings shalom. Simeon's prophecy (2:32) identifies Jesus as 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles' (phos eis apokalypsin ethnon) -- the light that will penetrate the darkness covering the nations under the governance of the rebellious elohim.

sections.heading: Annunciations: Gabriel's Messages to Zechariah and Mary (1:1-56)
sections.body: Luke's prologue (1:1-4) is a masterpiece of Hellenistic historiographic style, dedicating the work to Theophilus. The narrative begins in the Temple -- the place where heaven and earth intersect. Zechariah, an elderly priest, is performing the incense offering when Gabriel appears (1:11-20). Gabriel's message: Elizabeth will bear a son named John, who will 'go before him in the spirit and power of Elijah' (1:17). Zechariah doubts and is struck mute. Six months later, Gabriel appears to Mary in Nazareth: 'Greetings, O favored one (kecharitomene), the Lord is with you!' (1:28). The message: she will conceive by the Holy Spirit and bear a son named Jesus. 'He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High' (1:32). 'Of his kingdom there will be no end' (1:33). Mary's response: 'Behold, I am the servant (doule) of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word' (1:38). Mary visits Elizabeth; John leaps in the womb (1:41). Mary sings the Magnificat: 'My soul magnifies the Lord... he has scattered the proud... he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty' (1:46-55).

sections.heading: The Birth, the Shepherds, and the Temple Encounters (1:57 - 2:52)
sections.body: John is born and Zechariah sings the Benedictus (1:68-79): 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people and has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David.' The Benedictus is a covenant hymn: God remembers 'his holy covenant, the oath that he swore to our father Abraham' (1:72-73). Jesus is born in Bethlehem because of Augustus' census -- the emperor unwittingly fulfills Micah 5:2. Angels announce to shepherds (social outcasts, unable to observe purity laws) that 'a Savior has been born' (2:11). The 'multitude of the heavenly host' (2:13) -- the divine council's army -- praises God. Jesus is circumcised on the eighth day (2:21). At the Temple, Simeon takes the child and prophesies: 'My eyes have seen your salvation... a light for revelation to the Gentiles' (2:30-32). He also warns Mary: 'A sword will pierce through your own soul also' (2:35). Anna the prophetess 'spoke of him to all who were waiting for the redemption of Jerusalem' (2:38). At age 12, Jesus stays behind in the Temple, discussing with the teachers: 'Did you not know that I must (dei -- divine necessity) be in my Father's house?' (2:49). The inclusio is set: Luke begins and ends in the Temple.

luke_galilee.title: Baptism, Genealogy, and the Nazareth Sermon -- Satan's Claim and YHWH's Anointed
luke_galilee.synopsis: Luke sets the stage with precise historical synchronization (3:1-2), placing John's ministry in world-historical context. Jesus is baptized (3:21-22), the heavens open, the Spirit descends, and the Father declares: 'You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased.' Luke uniquely places the genealogy after the baptism (3:23-38), tracing Jesus' lineage all the way back to 'Adam, the son of God' (3:38) -- emphasizing Jesus as the representative of all humanity, not just Israel. The temptation (4:1-13) includes Luke's distinctive addition: Satan claims 'all this authority has been delivered to me' (4:6) -- the Deuteronomy 32 worldview explicit. The Nazareth sermon (4:16-30) is the programmatic statement of the entire Gospel: Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1-2 ('The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor'), declares 'Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing' (4:21), and provokes a violent reaction when he points to YHWH's grace extending to Gentiles (the widow of Zarephath and Naaman the Syrian, 4:25-27).

key_verse.text: And the devil took him up and showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time, and said to him, 'To you I will give all this authority and their glory, for it has been delivered to me, and I give it to whom I will.'

original_terms: emoi paradedotai (it has been delivered to me -- Satan's claim of delegated authority over the nations; passive voice implying God as the ultimate source; the Deut 32 worldview)
pneuma kyriou (Spirit of the Lord -- the anointing Spirit of Isaiah 61:1; Jesus is the Spirit-anointed Messiah who proclaims the jubilee)
aphesis (release / forgiveness -- 'to proclaim release to the captives' (4:18); jubilee language from Leviticus 25; liberation from bondage)
dektos (acceptable / favorable -- 'the year of the Lord's favor' (4:19, from Isa 61:2); the jubilee year when debts are canceled and slaves freed)
huios tou theou (Son of God -- the genealogy ends with 'Adam, the son of God' (3:38); Jesus is the true Son who succeeds where Adam failed)
luke_galilee.ane_backdrop: The Nazareth sermon's quotation of Isaiah 61:1-2 invokes the concept of the jubilee year (Leviticus 25) -- the year of release when slaves were freed, debts canceled, and land returned to original owners. In the ANE, royal edicts of 'release' (Akkadian andurarum, cognate to Hebrew deror, 'liberty') were proclaimed by new kings at their accession -- releasing prisoners, canceling debts, and restoring order. Jesus' Nazareth declaration is his accession announcement: the true King has arrived, and his reign means liberty for the captives. The genealogy tracing to Adam (3:38) sets Jesus in the broadest possible context: he is not merely the Messiah of Israel but the representative of all humanity, the second Adam who will succeed where the first failed.

second_temple.summary: This Qumran text applies Isaiah 61:1-2 to the eschatological jubilee, when Melchizedek will proclaim release, execute judgment against Belial and the spirits of his lot, and establish God's kingdom.
second_temple.relevance: Shows that Isaiah 61's jubilee was interpreted eschatologically at Qumran -- the same text Jesus claims to fulfill in the Nazareth sermon.

cross_refs.note: 'The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me to bring good news to the poor' -- the text Jesus reads and declares fulfilled
The nations allotted to the sons of God -- the theological background to Satan's claim of authority over 'all the kingdoms of the world' (4:5-6)
Elijah and the widow of Zarephath -- Jesus' example of YHWH's grace to Gentiles (4:25-26) that provokes Nazareth's fury
Naaman the Syrian healed of leprosy -- Jesus' second example (4:27) of YHWH's grace to Gentiles bypassing Israel
The jubilee legislation: release of slaves, cancellation of debts -- the background to the 'year of the Lord's favor' (4:19)
Matthew's temptation -- same three tests in different order; Matthew places the kingdoms temptation last; Luke places it second

luke_galilee.divine_council_note: Luke 4:5-6 is the most explicit divine council text in the temptation narratives. Satan shows Jesus 'all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time' (en stigme chronou, 4:5) -- a supernatural display of the entire network of national authorities under his control. His claim is specific: 'To you I will give all this authority (exousia) and their glory (doxa), for it has been delivered to me (emoi paradedotai), and I give it to whom I will' (4:6). The passive voice paradedotai ('has been delivered') implies a deliverer -- God himself, through the Deuteronomy 32 allotment at Babel. Satan does not claim to have seized this authority by force; he claims it was 'delivered' to him. In the Deut 32 framework, the nations were assigned to the bene elohim at Babel, and these spiritual rulers have corrupted their governance. Satan, as the chief adversary, claims to exercise authority over the entire network of territorial spirits. His offer is a genuine shortcut: worship me and receive the nations without the cross. Jesus refuses. The Nazareth sermon (4:16-30) is the counter-proclamation: the Spirit-anointed Messiah announces the jubilee -- the release of captives from the bondage of sin, disease, and the hostile powers. When Jesus mentions YHWH's grace to Gentiles (Zarephath, Naaman), the Nazareth crowd tries to kill him (4:28-29) -- because the implication is that the kingdom is not just for Israel. The nations that Satan claims to control are being reclaimed by grace.

sections.heading: Baptism, Genealogy, and Temptation (3:1 - 4:13)
sections.body: Luke sets the scene with six-fold historical synchronization: 'In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee...' (3:1-2). John preaches repentance and gives practical ethical instruction (3:10-14). Jesus is baptized while praying (a Lukan addition: Jesus often prays at key moments). The heavens open, the Spirit descends, and the voice declares: 'You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased' (3:22). Luke's genealogy (3:23-38) goes backward from Jesus through David and Abraham to 'Adam, the son of God (huios theou)' -- Jesus is the new Adam, the true Son. In the temptation (4:1-13), Luke places the kingdoms temptation second (Matthew has it third). Satan's explicit claim: 'all this authority has been delivered to me' (4:6). Jesus responds with Deuteronomy 6:8 and 6:12-13. Luke uniquely notes that the devil 'departed from him until an opportune time (achri kairou)' (4:13) -- the battle is not over; Satan will return, particularly in the passion (22:3, 31, 53).

sections.heading: The Nazareth Sermon: The Kingdom's Manifesto (4:14-30)
sections.body: Jesus returns to Nazareth 'in the power of the Spirit' (4:14). In the synagogue on the Sabbath, he reads from the scroll of Isaiah: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor. He has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor' (4:18-19, quoting Isa 61:1-2). He rolls up the scroll and sits down. Every eye is fixed on him. 'Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing (en tois osin hymon)' (4:21). The crowd marvels at first. But Jesus presses: 'No prophet is acceptable in his hometown' (4:24). Then the devastating comparison: Elijah was sent not to any widow in Israel but to a Gentile widow in Zarephath; Elisha healed not any Israelite leper but Naaman the Syrian (4:25-27). The implication: God's grace is not confined to Israel; the kingdom extends to the nations. 'When they heard these things, all in the synagogue were filled with wrath (thymou)' (4:28). They try to throw Jesus off a cliff, but 'passing through their midst, he went away' (4:30) -- the Messiah cannot be killed before his appointed time.

luke_galilee.title: Calling Disciples, Healing, and the Sermon on the Plain
luke_galilee.synopsis: Jesus calls his first disciples with a miraculous catch of fish (5:1-11), heals a leper and a paralytic (forgiving sins, 5:17-26), calls Levi the tax collector (5:27-32), and teaches about fasting, new wine, and new wineskins (5:33-39). He appoints the Twelve after a night of prayer (6:12-16) and delivers the Sermon on the Plain (6:17-49) -- Luke's parallel to Matthew's Sermon on the Mount. Luke's version is shorter, more direct, and includes both blessings and woes: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God' (6:20) paired with 'Woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation' (6:24). The ethic of enemy-love reaches its peak: 'Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you' (6:27-28). The sermon concludes with the parable of the two foundations (6:46-49).

key_verse.text: And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God. Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied.'

original_terms: ptochoi (poor -- Luke's Beatitudes address the literally poor, not just the 'poor in spirit' (Matt 5:3); Luke emphasizes material reversal)
ouai (woe -- Luke's unique woes (6:24-26) pronounce judgment on the rich, the full, the laughing, and the popular; the covenant curses inverted)
agapate tous echthrous (love your enemies -- the radical ethic of the kingdom; extending love beyond natural boundaries into enemy territory)
aphiemi (forgive / release -- 'forgive, and you will be forgiven' (6:37); the jubilee ethic applied to human relationships)
didaskale (teacher -- the crowds and disciples address Jesus as 'Teacher'; in Luke, Jesus is the authoritative interpreter of Torah)
luke_galilee.ane_backdrop: The miraculous catch of fish (5:1-11) has parallels in ancient literature where a deity or holy man demonstrates power over nature to call followers. The Sermon on the Plain's blessings and woes follow the covenant pattern of Deuteronomy 27-28: blessings for obedience, curses for disobedience. Luke's version sharpens the social dimension: the poor are blessed, the rich are cursed -- a reversal of the assumed social order that echoes the Magnificat (1:52-53). The command to love enemies was radical in the ancient world, where the duty of revenge was a fundamental social obligation. Neither Greek nor Roman ethics demanded love of enemies; the Qumran community explicitly required hatred of outsiders (1QS 1:9-10).

second_temple.summary: Members are instructed 'to love all that He has chosen and to hate all that He has rejected... to love all the sons of light... and to hate all the sons of darkness.'
second_temple.relevance: Provides a direct contrast to Jesus' command to 'love your enemies' -- even the most devout Jewish community at Qumran did not extend love beyond its boundaries.

cross_refs.note: The Sermon on the Mount -- Matthew's longer parallel; 'poor in spirit' vs. Luke's 'poor'; no woes in Matthew
Covenant blessings and curses -- the literary form behind Luke's blessings and woes
'Good news to the poor' -- the Nazareth manifesto now realized: the poor receive the kingdom
'Love your neighbor as yourself' -- Jesus extends this beyond 'neighbor' to 'enemy'
'Has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom?' -- echoing Luke's Beatitude

luke_galilee.divine_council_note: The Sermon on the Plain describes kingdom life as the reversal of the present world order -- an order maintained by the hostile powers. The poor are blessed because the kingdom belongs to them; the rich are warned because their wealth is tied to the systems of the present age. The command to love enemies (6:27-36) is not mere ethics but cosmic strategy: the kingdom advances not through the power dynamics of the present age (retaliation, domination, self-preservation) but through the radical self-giving love that characterizes the Father himself ('he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil,' 6:35). This ethic mirrors the divine council's character: YHWH extends grace even to the nations under rebellious rulers. The promise 'your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High (huioi hypsistou)' (6:35) uses the divine council title: those who love their enemies are identified as children of the Most High God -- participants in the divine family, sharing the character of the God who rules the council.

sections.heading: Miraculous Catch, Healings, and Calling Levi (5:1-39)
sections.body: Jesus teaches from Simon's boat, then commands him to cast nets into the deep. The catch is so great the nets begin to break (5:6). Peter falls at Jesus' knees: 'Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord' (5:8) -- a response to the numinous, the experience of divine holiness. Jesus: 'Do not be afraid; from now on you will be catching men' (5:10). They leave everything and follow. A leper is healed: 'I will; be clean' (5:13). A paralytic is lowered through the roof; Jesus says 'Man, your sins are forgiven you' (5:20), then heals the paralysis to prove his authority to forgive. Levi the tax collector is called from his booth (5:27-28). At Levi's banquet with 'a great company of tax collectors and others,' Jesus declares: 'I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance' (5:32).

sections.heading: The Twelve Appointed and the Sermon on the Plain (6:1-49)
sections.body: After Sabbath controversies (6:1-11), Jesus spends the night in prayer on a mountain (6:12) and appoints the Twelve (6:13-16). Coming down to 'a level place' (6:17), he delivers the Sermon on the Plain. Four blessings: blessed are the poor, the hungry, the weeping, and the hated (6:20-23). Four woes: woe to the rich, the full, the laughing, and the popular (6:24-26). The central teaching: 'Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you' (6:27). 'If someone strikes you on one cheek, offer the other also' (6:29). 'Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful' (6:36). 'Judge not, and you will not be judged' (6:37). The log and speck (6:41-42). 'A good tree does not bear bad fruit' (6:43). The two builders: 'Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and does them... is like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid the foundation on the rock' (6:47-48). The one who does not is a house without a foundation that collapses when the flood strikes (6:49).

luke_galilee.title: Faith of Gentiles, Raising the Dead, and Parables of the Kingdom
luke_galilee.synopsis: Chapters 7-8 demonstrate the kingdom's power reaching beyond expected boundaries. A Roman centurion's faith astounds Jesus: 'I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such faith' (7:9). Jesus raises the widow of Nain's son (7:11-17) -- a miracle unique to Luke that echoes Elijah's raising of the widow's son in Zarephath (1 Kings 17:17-24). John the Baptist sends his question, and Jesus responds with the Isaiah 35/61 evidence (7:18-23). A sinful woman anoints Jesus' feet (7:36-50) -- forgiven because she loved much. Jesus teaches in parables (8:4-18): the Sower, with its emphasis on hearing and doing. Women support the ministry financially: Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Susanna, and 'many others' (8:1-3) -- a detail unique to Luke, reflecting his concern for women's roles. The storm is stilled, the Gerasene demoniac is liberated, Jairus' daughter is raised, and the hemorrhaging woman is healed (8:22-56).

key_verse.text: When Jesus heard these things, he marveled at him, and turning to the crowd that followed him, said, 'I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such faith.'

original_terms: pistis (faith -- the centurion's faith surpasses Israel's; faith is the operative response to the kingdom's arrival)
prophetes megas (a great prophet -- the crowd's response to the raising of the widow's son: 'A great prophet has arisen among us!' (7:16); echoing Elijah)
epeskepsato (has visited -- 'God has visited his people!' (7:16); the same verb used in 1:68 (Benedictus); YHWH's personal intervention in history)
aphientai (are forgiven -- 'Her sins, which are many, are forgiven (aphientai) -- for she loved much' (7:47); the perfect passive indicates divine action)
luke_galilee.ane_backdrop: The centurion's faith (7:1-10) involves the concept of delegated authority: 'I say to my servant, "Do this," and he does it' (7:8). The centurion recognizes that Jesus operates within a chain of command -- like a military officer, Jesus has authority from above and can command spiritual forces with a word. This is an implicit recognition of the divine council hierarchy: Jesus exercises authority delegated by the Most High. The raising at Nain (7:11-17) parallels Elijah's miracle in 1 Kings 17:17-24 -- both involve a widow's only son in a town in the north. The crowd's response -- 'A great prophet has arisen!' -- echoes the Deuteronomy 18:15 expectation of a prophet like Moses.

second_temple.summary: When God's Messiah comes, 'the heavens and the earth will listen to his Messiah... He will heal the wounded, revive the dead, and proclaim good news to the poor.'
second_temple.relevance: The catalog of messianic signs matches Jesus' answer to John: the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news preached (7:22).

cross_refs.note: Elijah raises the widow of Zarephath's son -- the parallel to Jesus raising the widow of Nain's son (7:11-17)
The messianic signs catalog -- the blind see, the deaf hear, the poor hear good news -- cited in Jesus' answer to John (7:22)
Matthew's parallel centurion story -- similar dialogue but different framing; Matthew adds the 'many from east and west' saying
Mark's parallel parables, storm stilling, Gerasene demoniac, and Jairus' daughter -- Luke follows Mark's order closely

luke_galilee.divine_council_note: The centurion's faith (7:1-10) has profound divine council implications. The centurion -- a Gentile military officer serving the occupying power -- recognizes Jesus' authority as operating within a hierarchical chain of command. Just as the centurion commands soldiers and servants by virtue of the authority above him, Jesus commands disease and demons by virtue of the authority of the Most High. The centurion's insight into Jesus' authority surpasses all of Israel's understanding -- a Gentile, from the territory of the nations, recognizes the divine council's authority in Jesus. This foreshadows the Gentile mission: the nations will receive the kingdom's good news and respond with faith. The raising at Nain (7:11-17) demonstrates authority over death itself -- the ultimate enemy, the domain that Sheol and Hades claim as their own. Jesus invades death's territory and brings back a captive.

sections.heading: The Centurion, the Widow, and John's Question (7:1-35)
sections.body: A centurion in Capernaum sends Jewish elders to Jesus, asking him to heal his servant. The elders commend the centurion: 'He is worthy... he loves our nation, and he is the one who built us our synagogue' (7:4-5). As Jesus approaches, the centurion sends friends: 'Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy to have you come under my roof... say the word, and let my servant be healed. For I too am a man set under authority' (7:6-8). Jesus marvels: 'Not even in Israel have I found such faith' (7:9). At Nain, Jesus encounters a funeral procession for the only son of a widow (7:11-12). 'He had compassion (esplanchnisthe) on her' (7:13) and says 'Young man, I say to you, arise' (7:14). The dead man sits up and begins to speak (7:15). The crowd: 'A great prophet has arisen among us!' and 'God has visited (epeskepsato) his people!' (7:16). John sends his question: 'Are you the one who is to come?' (7:19). Jesus responds with evidence: the blind see, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor hear good news (7:22).

sections.heading: The Sinful Woman, the Women Disciples, and Miracles (7:36 - 8:56)
sections.body: At a Pharisee's house, a 'woman of the city, who was a sinner' (7:37) wets Jesus' feet with tears, wipes them with her hair, and anoints them with ointment. The Pharisee thinks: 'If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what sort of woman this is' (7:39). Jesus tells the parable of the two debtors (7:41-43) and declares: 'Her sins, which are many, are forgiven -- for she loved much' (7:47). Chapter 8 notes the women who support Jesus' ministry: Mary Magdalene ('from whom seven demons had gone out'), Joanna (wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager), Susanna, and 'many others' (8:1-3). Jesus teaches the parable of the Sower (8:4-15), then demonstrates authority over nature (the storm stilled, 8:22-25), over a legion of demons (the Gerasene demoniac, 8:26-39), over disease (the hemorrhaging woman, 8:43-48), and over death (Jairus' daughter, 8:49-56). The authority proclaimed in the parables is confirmed in the miracles.

luke_galilee.title: The Twelve Sent, Peter's Confession, Transfiguration, and the Turn to Jerusalem
luke_galilee.synopsis: Chapter 9 is the hinge of Luke's Gospel. Jesus sends the Twelve with 'power and authority over all demons and to cure diseases' (9:1). He feeds the 5,000 (9:10-17). Peter confesses: 'The Christ of God' (9:20). Jesus predicts his death (9:22), teaches the cost of discipleship (9:23-27), and is transfigured on the mountain (9:28-36). Luke uniquely records that Moses and Elijah 'spoke of his departure (exodon), which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem' (9:31) -- the word exodos ('exodus/departure') links Jesus' death to Israel's liberation from Egypt. After healing a demon-possessed boy (9:37-43), Jesus predicts his death again (9:44-45), teaches about true greatness (9:46-48), and then the critical turning point: 'When the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem' (9:51). The Galilean ministry is over. The journey to the cross begins.

key_verse.text: [They] appeared in glory and spoke of his departure (exodon), which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.

original_terms: exodos (departure / exodus -- Moses and Elijah discuss Jesus' 'exodus' at Jerusalem; linking the crucifixion to Israel's liberation from bondage; Jesus' death is the new exodus)
Christos tou theou (the Christ of God -- Peter's confession; the anointed one who belongs to God; the long-awaited Messiah)
to prosopon esterisen (he set his face -- 'he set his face to go to Jerusalem' (9:51); echoing Isaiah 50:7, the Servant's resolve: 'I have set my face like a flint')
analempsis (taking up -- 'the days drew near for him to be taken up' (9:51); referring to the ascension; the entire journey is oriented toward his 'taking up' into heaven)
doxa (glory -- Moses and Elijah 'appeared in glory' (9:31); Jesus' face 'altered' and his clothing 'dazzling white' (9:29); the divine glory unveiled)
luke_galilee.ane_backdrop: The Transfiguration's connection to the exodus motif is profound. Moses and Elijah -- the two figures most associated with Sinai/Horeb -- appear to discuss Jesus' 'exodus' (exodon, 9:31). The first exodus liberated Israel from Egypt through blood (Passover lamb) and water (Red Sea). Jesus' exodus will liberate humanity from the bondage of sin and the hostile powers through his death (the blood of the new covenant) and resurrection (the passage through death to new life). Luke's use of the word exodos is deliberate: the entire Lukan passion narrative is a new exodus, with Jesus as both the new Moses (leading the people to freedom) and the Passover lamb (whose blood effects liberation).

second_temple.summary: Peter uses the same word exodos for his own impending death: 'I will make every effort so that after my exodus (exodon) you may be able at any time to recall these things.'
second_temple.relevance: Confirms that the Transfiguration's exodus language shaped early Christian understanding of death as a 'departure' that leads to glory.

cross_refs.note: 'I have set my face like a flint' -- the Servant's resolve echoed in Jesus setting his face toward Jerusalem (9:51)
The original exodus: liberation through blood and water -- the background to Jesus' 'exodus' discussed at the Transfiguration
'Listen to him' -- the command at the Transfiguration (9:35) echoes Moses' prophecy of the prophet like him
Mark's parallel: Peter's confession, passion prediction, and Transfiguration -- Mark does not mention the 'exodus' discussion
Matthew's parallel: adds the rock/keys saying, 'gates of Hades' declaration, and 'flesh and blood' revelation

luke_galilee.divine_council_note: Luke's Transfiguration account adds the crucial detail that Moses and Elijah 'appeared in glory (doxa)' (9:31) and discussed Jesus' 'exodus (exodon), which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.' This is a divine council deliberation: the representatives of the Law and the Prophets, appearing in divine glory, discuss the plan of salvation with the Son of God on the mountain. The word exodon is Luke's theological key: just as the first exodus was YHWH's definitive act of liberation from the powers that held Israel in bondage (Egypt, Pharaoh, the gods of Egypt), Jesus' death-resurrection-ascension will be YHWH's definitive act of liberation from the powers that hold all humanity in bondage (sin, death, Satan, and the rebellious elohim). The voice from the cloud -- 'This is my Son, my Chosen One (ho eklelegmenos mou); listen to him' (9:35) -- uses the language of Isaiah 42:1 (the Chosen Servant) and Deuteronomy 18:15 (the prophet like Moses). The turning point at 9:51 -- 'he set his face to go to Jerusalem' -- is the Son's resolve to accomplish the exodus that the council has decreed. The journey that follows (9:51-19:44) is the march toward the cross, the exodus event that will liberate the nations.

sections.heading: The Twelve Sent, Feeding, and Peter's Confession (9:1-27)
sections.body: Jesus gives the Twelve 'power and authority (dynamin kai exousian) over all demons and to cure diseases' (9:1). They go out preaching and healing (9:6). Herod is perplexed, hearing reports that John has been raised or that Elijah or a prophet has appeared (9:7-9). Jesus feeds 5,000 with five loaves and two fish (9:10-17). While praying alone (a Lukan detail), Jesus asks: 'Who do the crowds say that I am?' (9:18). Peter answers: 'The Christ of God (ton Christon tou theou)' (9:20). Jesus commands secrecy and predicts: 'The Son of Man must (dei) suffer many things and be rejected... and be killed, and on the third day be raised' (9:22). 'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily (kath hemeran -- a Lukan addition: daily cross-bearing) and follow me' (9:23).

sections.heading: The Transfiguration and the Turn to Jerusalem (9:28-62)
sections.body: About eight days later (Luke's timing differs from Mark's 'six days'), Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up the mountain 'to pray' (9:28 -- another Lukan detail). While praying, 'the appearance of his face was altered, and his clothing became dazzling white' (9:29). Moses and Elijah appear 'in glory' and discuss his 'exodus' (exodon) to be accomplished at Jerusalem (9:31). Peter, James, and John are 'heavy with sleep' but awaken to see the glory (9:32). Peter offers to build three tabernacles (9:33). A cloud overshadows them, and the voice says: 'This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him' (9:35). The next day, Jesus heals a demon-possessed boy the disciples could not heal (9:37-43). He predicts his death a second time (9:44). The disciples argue about greatness; Jesus places a child beside him (9:46-48). Then the decisive verse: 'When the days drew near for him to be taken up (analempsis), he set his face (to prosopon esterisen) to go to Jerusalem' (9:51). The journey begins. He encounters would-be followers: 'Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (9:58). 'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God' (9:62).

luke_jerusalem.title: The Seventy-Two Sent, Satan Falls, and the Parables of Grace
luke_jerusalem.synopsis: The Travel Narrative opens with the sending of the 72 (or 70) -- a symbolic mission to the nations (echoing the 70/72 nations of Gen 10 LXX). They return rejoicing: 'Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name!' (10:17). Jesus responds: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven' (10:18). He gives authority 'over all the power of the enemy' (10:19) but says the greater joy is that 'your names are written in heaven' (10:20). The Good Samaritan (10:25-37) redefines 'neighbor' across ethnic boundaries. Martha and Mary (10:38-42) illustrate the priority of listening to Jesus. Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer (11:1-4), the parable of the persistent friend (11:5-8), and responds to the Beelzebul accusation with the strong man parable (11:14-26). Woes against the Pharisees and lawyers follow (11:37-54). Chapter 12 teaches about fear, anxiety, and readiness for the Son of Man's return. Chapter 13 contains the call to repentance, the parable of the barren fig tree, the healing of a bent woman on the Sabbath, and the narrow door teaching.

key_verse.text: And he said to them, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven. Behold, I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy, and nothing shall hurt you. Nevertheless, do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.'

original_terms: Satanas (Satan -- the adversary; Jesus sees him falling from his position of heavenly authority as the 72 advance into the nations' territory)
exousia (authority -- given to the 72 to 'tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power (dynamin) of the enemy' (10:19); delegated divine council authority)
onomata hymon engegraptai en tois ouranois (your names are written in heaven -- registration in the divine council's book of life; citizenship in the heavenly realm)
plesion (neighbor -- the Good Samaritan's redefinition: 'neighbor' is not defined by ethnicity but by mercy; the Deuteronomy 32 boundaries of Israel/nations are transcended)
opheis kai skorpious (serpents and scorpions -- cosmic symbols of evil and hostile powers; Gen 3:15's serpent; Deut 8:15's wilderness dangers; the spiritual enemies)
luke_jerusalem.ane_backdrop: The mission of the 72 draws on the Table of Nations tradition (Gen 10), which in the LXX lists 72 nations (the MT has 70). At Babel (Gen 11), these nations were scattered and, according to Deuteronomy 32:8-9, allotted to the sons of God. Jesus' sending of 72 envoys is a deliberate symbolic reversal: where YHWH scattered the nations and assigned them to lesser elohim, the Son now sends representatives to reclaim them. 'Serpents and scorpions' (10:19) symbolize hostile spiritual powers in the ANE: the serpent of Eden (Gen 3), the fiery serpents of the wilderness (Num 21:6), and the scorpions of divine judgment (Deut 8:15; Ezek 2:6). Authority to 'tread on' them echoes Psalm 91:13: 'You will tread on the lion and the adder; the young lion and the serpent you will trample underfoot.' The 'book of life' concept (names written in heaven) has Mesopotamian parallels: the Tablets of Destiny, on which the fates of gods and humans are inscribed by the divine council.

second_temple.summary: The Son of Man is enthroned over the spirits of the fallen angels. Their names are written in a book; judgment is executed according to the heavenly records.
second_temple.relevance: The concept of heavenly registration ('your names are written in heaven') is paralleled by the Enochic tradition of cosmic bookkeeping -- names recorded in the divine council's archives.

cross_refs.note: The Table of Nations: 70 (MT) or 72 (LXX) nations -- the background to sending 72 envoys to symbolically reclaim the nations
The nations allotted to the sons of God -- the worldview behind both Satan's claim (4:6) and the 72 mission (10:1)
'You will tread on the lion and the adder' -- the authority over 'serpents and scorpions' given to the 72
'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn!' -- echoed in 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven'
The protoevangelium: the seed of the woman will crush the serpent's head -- the authority to 'tread on serpents' fulfills this
Satan thrown down from heaven -- the eschatological fulfillment of what Jesus saw beginning in the 72's mission
Matthew's mission discourse for the Twelve -- Luke has both the Twelve (9:1-6) and the 72 (10:1-20)

luke_jerusalem.divine_council_note: Luke 10:17-20 is one of the most important divine council passages in the Gospels. As the 72 advance into the territory of the nations -- symbolically reversing the Babel scattering of Genesis 10-11 and Deuteronomy 32:8-9 -- Jesus sees Satan 'fall like lightning from heaven' (10:18). The verb theoro ('I was watching') is imperfect tense, suggesting ongoing observation: as the mission progresses, Jesus watches the cosmic consequences unfold. Satan's 'fall' is from his position of authority over the nations -- the same authority he claimed in 4:6. As the nations are reclaimed for YHWH through the gospel, Satan loses his position in the heavenly hierarchy. The authority given to the disciples -- 'to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power (dynamin) of the enemy (echthrou)' (10:19) -- is cosmic warfare language: serpents and scorpions represent the hostile spiritual powers, and the 'enemy' is Satan himself. But the climax is 10:20: 'Rejoice that your names are written (engegraptai, perfect tense -- permanently inscribed) in heaven.' The names of Jesus' followers are registered in the divine council's records -- the heavenly book of life (Exod 32:32; Dan 12:1; Phil 4:3; Rev 3:5). The Good Samaritan parable (10:25-37) then demonstrates what kingdom life looks like: mercy that transcends the ethnic and religious boundaries erected by the Deuteronomy 32 order. A Samaritan (despised by Jews as a mixed-race apostate) shows mercy when the priest and Levite do not. The kingdom redefines 'neighbor' across all human divisions.

sections.heading: The Seventy-Two, Satan's Fall, and the Good Samaritan (10:1-42)
sections.body: Jesus appoints 72 others and sends them 'two by two ahead of him into every town and place where he himself was about to go' (10:1). Instructions echo the Twelve's mission: travel light, eat what is set before you, heal the sick, proclaim 'The kingdom of God has come near to you' (10:9). Towns that reject them face judgment worse than Sodom's (10:12). The 72 return: 'Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name!' (10:17). Jesus: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning (astrapen) from heaven' (10:18). He gives authority over the enemy's power (10:19) but redirects their joy: 'rejoice that your names are written in heaven' (10:20). Jesus then rejoices 'in the Holy Spirit' (10:21, unique to Luke): 'I thank you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little children.' A lawyer asks 'Who is my neighbor?' (10:29). The Good Samaritan parable follows: a man beaten and left for dead; a priest and a Levite pass by; a Samaritan shows mercy. 'Which of these three proved to be a neighbor?' The lawyer answers: 'The one who showed him mercy.' Jesus: 'Go and do likewise' (10:37).

sections.heading: Beelzebul, Woes, Fear, and Readiness (11:1 - 13:35)
sections.body: Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer in a shorter form than Matthew's (11:2-4). The Beelzebul controversy (11:14-26): 'If I cast out demons by the finger of God (daktyloi theou, cf. Exod 8:19), then the kingdom of God has come upon you' (11:20). Luke uses 'finger of God' where Matthew has 'Spirit of God' -- the finger of God is the power that performed the Egyptian plagues (Exod 8:19), connecting Jesus' exorcisms to the exodus. Woes against Pharisees and lawyers (11:37-54). Chapter 12: 'Do not fear those who kill the body... Fear him who, after he has killed, has authority to cast into hell' (12:4-5). 'Are not five sparrows sold for two pennies? And not one of them is forgotten before God' (12:6). The rich fool parable (12:13-21): 'Fool! This night your soul is required of you.' 'Do not be anxious about your life... Seek his kingdom, and these things will be added to you' (12:22-31). 'You also must be ready, for the Son of Man is coming at an hour you do not expect' (12:40). Chapter 13: repentance (13:1-5), the barren fig tree (13:6-9), the bent woman healed on the Sabbath (13:10-17). The lament: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets!' (13:34).

luke_jerusalem.title: The Cost of Discipleship and the Parables of the Lost
luke_jerusalem.synopsis: These chapters contain some of Luke's most beloved parables. The great banquet (14:15-24): those first invited refuse, so the host invites 'the poor and crippled and blind and lame' (14:21) and then compels people from 'the highways and hedges' (14:23) -- the outcasts and Gentiles fill the kingdom. The cost of discipleship: 'Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple' (14:27). Chapter 15 is the heart of Luke: three parables of the lost -- the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son (15:1-32). The father's lavish welcome of the rebellious son is the supreme picture of divine grace. Chapter 16 contains the shrewd manager and the rich man and Lazarus (16:19-31) -- afterlife reversal and the sufficiency of 'Moses and the Prophets.' Chapter 17 teaches on forgiveness, faith, and the coming of the kingdom: 'The kingdom of God is in the midst of you' (17:21).

key_verse.text: And he arose and came to his father. But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him.

original_terms: splanchnistheis (felt compassion -- the father's visceral response to the prodigal; splanchna are the 'innards,' the seat of deep emotion in Greek; divine compassion made human)
apollymi (to lose / to perish / to destroy -- the key verb of chapter 15: the sheep is 'lost,' the coin is 'lost,' the son 'was lost'; God seeks the lost)
metanoia (repentance -- 'there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance' (15:7); cosmic celebration in the divine council)
entos hymon (in the midst of you / within you -- 'the kingdom of God is in the midst of you' (17:21); the kingdom is present in Jesus' person, not a future-only reality)
luke_jerusalem.ane_backdrop: The prodigal son's journey to 'a far country' (15:13) where he feeds pigs -- unclean animals for a Jew -- represents complete apostasy and assimilation into the Gentile world. In the Deuteronomy 32 framework, this is a journey into the territory of the nations under the rebellious elohim. The father's response -- running to meet him, which was undignified for a patriarch in ANE culture -- represents YHWH's scandalous grace that crosses every boundary to welcome the returning sinner. The rich man and Lazarus (16:19-31) reflects the Jewish concept of differentiated afterlife: Abraham's bosom (paradise) and Hades (punishment), separated by an impassable chasm. The patriarch Abraham's presence in the afterlife scene connects to the ancient concept of ancestors as mediators in the spiritual realm.

second_temple.summary: Enoch sees the chambers of the dead divided into sections: one for the righteous (with a spring of water and light), and separate chambers for different categories of the wicked (in darkness).
second_temple.relevance: Provides the Second Temple background for the Rich Man and Lazarus: differentiated afterlife, separate chambers, and the inability to cross between them.

cross_refs.note: 'I myself will search for my sheep, and will seek them out' -- the divine shepherd background to the parable of the lost sheep
'When Israel was a child, I loved him... How can I give you up, O Ephraim?' -- YHWH's parental compassion reflected in the prodigal's father
The law of the rebellious son -- the prodigal would deserve this punishment; the father's grace overrides the law's severity
Matthew's lost sheep parable -- in Matthew, the context is community care for 'little ones'; in Luke, the context is the Pharisees criticizing Jesus' association with sinners
'While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' -- Paul's theology of prevenient grace, embodied in the father who runs to the prodigal

luke_jerusalem.divine_council_note: Chapter 15's three parables of the lost reveal the divine council's response to repentance. The shepherd who finds his lost sheep calls 'friends and neighbors' to rejoice -- 'there will be more joy in heaven (chara en to ouranoi) over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance' (15:7). 'Joy in heaven' is a reference to the divine council's celebration: when a sinner repents, the heavenly assembly rejoices. Similarly, 'there is joy before the angels of God (enopion ton angelon tou theou) over one sinner who repents' (15:10). The angels -- divine council members -- celebrate each act of repentance as a reclamation from enemy territory. The prodigal son parable is the fullest expression: the son who went to 'a far country' (the territory of the nations) and squandered everything returns, and the father's response is extravagant celebration -- the robe, the ring, the sandals, the fatted calf. This is the Deuteronomy 32 reversal at the personal level: one who was lost in the territory of the nations is reclaimed and restored to the father's house. The elder brother's resentment (15:25-32) mirrors the Pharisees' complaint (15:2) and represents those within Israel who refuse to celebrate the expansion of grace to sinners and outsiders.

sections.heading: The Great Banquet and the Cost (14:1-35)
sections.body: At a Pharisee's house, Jesus heals on the Sabbath (14:1-6), teaches on humility at banquets (14:7-14), and tells the parable of the great banquet (14:15-24). The invited guests make excuses; the host sends servants to bring in 'the poor and crippled and blind and lame' (14:21) and then compels others from 'the highways and hedges' (14:23). The parable declares: those who reject the kingdom invitation are replaced by the unexpected -- the outcasts, the sinners, the Gentiles. 'None of those men who were invited shall taste my banquet' (14:24). The cost: 'Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple' (14:27). 'Salt is good, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored?' (14:34).

sections.heading: The Three Lost Parables and the Rich Man and Lazarus (15:1 - 16:31)
sections.body: The Pharisees grumble: 'This man receives sinners and eats with them' (15:2). Jesus responds with three parables. The lost sheep (15:3-7): one sheep out of 100 is lost; the shepherd leaves the 99 and searches until he finds it. 'Joy in heaven over one sinner who repents.' The lost coin (15:8-10): one drachma out of ten is lost; the woman sweeps the house until she finds it. 'Joy before the angels of God.' The prodigal son (15:11-32): the younger son takes his inheritance, squanders it in a far country, hits bottom feeding pigs, and 'comes to himself' (15:17). He returns, planning to say 'I am no longer worthy to be called your son; treat me as one of your hired servants' (15:19). But the father runs to him, embraces him, gives him the best robe, a ring, and sandals, and kills the fatted calf. 'This my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found' (15:24). The elder brother is angry and refuses to enter. The father pleads: 'Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours. It was fitting to celebrate and be glad' (15:31-32). Chapter 16: the shrewd manager (16:1-13). The rich man and Lazarus (16:19-31): the rich man feasts while Lazarus begs at his gate; after death, Lazarus is in Abraham's bosom and the rich man in Hades. The rich man begs Abraham to warn his brothers; Abraham: 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them... If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone should rise from the dead' (16:29, 31).

luke_jerusalem.title: Jerusalem Approaches -- The Entry, the Temple, and the Olivet Discourse
luke_jerusalem.synopsis: The journey to Jerusalem nears its end. Jesus tells the parable of the persistent widow (18:1-8) and the Pharisee and tax collector (18:9-14): 'Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.' He blesses children, encounters the rich ruler (18:18-30), and predicts his death a third time (18:31-34). He heals blind Bartimaeus near Jericho (18:35-43). Zacchaeus the chief tax collector climbs a sycamore tree and receives Jesus: 'Today salvation has come to this house... For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost' (19:9-10). Jesus enters Jerusalem riding a colt (19:28-40), weeps over the city (19:41-44), and cleanses the Temple (19:45-48). Controversies follow (20:1-44), and the Olivet Discourse (21:5-36) warns of Jerusalem's destruction and the coming of the Son of Man: 'When you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near' (21:20).

key_verse.text: For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.

original_terms: soteria (salvation -- 'Today salvation has come to this house' (19:9); salvation as a present reality entering a specific household through Jesus' presence)
zeteo kai sosai to apollos (seek and save the lost -- the mission statement of the Son of Man; combining shepherd imagery (Ezek 34) with the 'lost' theme of chapter 15)
eirene (peace -- 'Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!' (19:42); the shalom that Jesus offers and Jerusalem rejects)
kairos (appointed time -- 'you did not know the time (kairon) of your visitation (episkopes)' (19:44); God's visit (episkope) had a window, and Jerusalem missed it)
luke_jerusalem.ane_backdrop: Jesus' weeping over Jerusalem (19:41-44) draws on the prophetic tradition of lament over the doomed city (Jer 6:26; 8:18-22; Lam 1-5). The language of siege -- 'your enemies will set up a barricade around you and surround you and hem you in on every side' (19:43) -- describes standard Roman siege warfare: circumvallation walls, starvation, and total destruction. Josephus' account of the 70 AD siege (War 5-6) describes exactly these tactics. The parable of the ten minas (19:11-27) reflects the historical practice of client kings traveling to Rome to receive their kingship from the emperor -- a known pattern with the Herodian dynasty (Archelaus went to Rome in 4 BC to receive his territory).

second_temple.summary: Josephus records signs that preceded Jerusalem's destruction: a star resembling a sword hung over the city, chariots and troops were seen in the sky, and a voice from the Temple said 'We are departing hence.'
second_temple.relevance: Luke 21:20-24 describes Jerusalem's coming destruction, which was fulfilled in 70 AD as Josephus records. The 'departing' voice echoes Ezekiel's departure of the shekinah and Jesus' 'your house is left desolate.'

cross_refs.note: 'I myself will search for my sheep' -- the background to 'the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost'
'Your king is coming to you... humble and mounted on a donkey' -- fulfilled in the triumphal entry (19:28-40)
The Son of Man coming with the clouds -- the background to 'they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory' (21:27)
'Den of robbers' -- quoted in the Temple cleansing (19:46)
Mark's parallel: triumphal entry, Temple cleansing, controversies, and Olivet Discourse
Matthew's parallel: adds the sheep/goats judgment, ten virgins, and talents parables

luke_jerusalem.divine_council_note: Jesus' weeping over Jerusalem (19:41-44) is a prophetic lament with divine council overtones. The phrase 'the time of your visitation' (ton kairon tes episkopes sou, 19:44) uses the word episkope -- 'visitation' -- which in the OT refers to YHWH's arrival to judge or save (Isa 10:3; Jer 6:15). God has visited his people in Jesus (the same word used in 1:68, 'he has visited and redeemed his people'), and Jerusalem has missed it. The Olivet Discourse (21:5-36) includes the cosmic signs: 'signs in sun and moon and stars, and on the earth distress of nations in perplexity' (21:25). 'The powers of the heavens will be shaken' (21:26) -- the spiritual authorities governing the cosmos are destabilized. 'Then they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory' (21:27). Luke uniquely adds: 'When these things begin to take place, straighten up and raise your heads, because your redemption is drawing near' (21:28) -- for the faithful, the cosmic upheaval is not terror but liberation. The old order governed by hostile powers is collapsing; the Son of Man's coming completes the reclamation.

sections.heading: Parables of Persistence, Zacchaeus, and the Entry (18:1 - 19:48)
sections.body: The persistent widow (18:1-8): 'And will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night?' (18:7). The Pharisee and tax collector (18:9-14): the tax collector, beating his breast -- 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner!' -- goes home justified (18:14). The rich ruler asks about eternal life; Jesus says 'Sell all that you have and distribute to the poor' (18:22); he goes away sad. Third passion prediction (18:31-34). Blind Bartimaeus healed (18:35-43). Zacchaeus in Jericho: a chief tax collector, rich, short in stature, climbs a sycamore tree (19:1-4). Jesus says: 'Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for I must (dei) stay at your house today' (19:5). Zacchaeus repents and gives half his goods to the poor. 'Today salvation (soteria) has come to this house' (19:9). 'The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost' (19:10). The parable of the ten minas (19:11-27). The triumphal entry (19:28-40): the disciples shout 'Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!' (19:38). Pharisees object; Jesus: 'If these were silent, the very stones would cry out' (19:40). He weeps over Jerusalem: 'If you had known the things that make for peace!' (19:42).

sections.heading: Temple Cleansing, Controversies, and the Olivet Discourse (20:1 - 21:38)
sections.body: Jesus cleanses the Temple: 'My house shall be a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers' (19:46). Controversies: the question of authority (20:1-8), the parable of the tenants (20:9-18), paying taxes to Caesar (20:20-26), the resurrection debate (20:27-40), and the Psalm 110 question: 'David calls him Lord, so how is he his son?' (20:41-44). The Olivet Discourse (21:5-36): 'As for these things that you see, the days will come when there will not be left here one stone upon another' (21:6). Wars, earthquakes, famines, persecutions (21:10-19). Luke's distinctive detail: 'When you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near' (21:20) -- more specific than Mark's 'abomination of desolation.' 'Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled' (21:24). Cosmic signs and the Son of Man coming in a cloud (21:25-27). 'Straighten up and raise your heads, because your redemption is drawing near' (21:28).

luke_jerusalem.title: Satan Returns -- The Last Supper, Trial, and Crucifixion
luke_jerusalem.synopsis: Luke's passion narrative is the most psychologically nuanced of the four Gospels. Satan, who 'departed until an opportune time' (4:13), now returns: 'Satan entered into Judas' (22:3). Jesus celebrates the Passover, institutes the Lord's Supper, and reveals that Satan has 'demanded to sift you like wheat' (22:31) -- divine council courtroom language. In Gethsemane, 'an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him' (22:43, unique to Luke). Jesus is arrested, Peter denies him three times, and Jesus 'turned and looked at Peter' (22:61, unique to Luke). Before the Sanhedrin, Jesus says: 'From now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God' (22:69). Before Pilate and Herod (only Luke includes the Herod trial, 23:6-12), he is condemned. On the way to Golgotha, he tells the weeping women: 'Do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children' (23:28). From the cross: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (23:34). To the penitent thief: 'Today you will be with me in paradise (paradeiso)' (23:43). His last words: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!' (23:46, quoting Psalm 31:5).

key_verse.text: Simon, Simon, behold, Satan demanded to have you, that he might sift you like wheat, but I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned again, strengthen your brothers.

original_terms: exetesato (demanded -- Satan's formal request before the divine council to test Peter; courtroom/legal language; echoing Job 1:6-12)
siniazo (to sift -- 'sift you like wheat' (22:31); vigorous shaking to separate grain from chaff; the testing will be violent and thorough)
paradeisos (paradise -- 'Today you will be with me in paradise' (23:43); the garden of God (Gen 2; Ezek 28:13); the restored Eden where the righteous dwell after death)
paratithemai (I commit / I entrust -- 'Into your hands I commit my spirit' (23:46, Ps 31:5); the Son entrusts himself to the Father; trust even unto death)
hora (hour -- 'this is your hour, and the power (exousia) of darkness' (22:53); the time when Satan's authority is temporarily unleashed against Jesus)
luke_jerusalem.ane_backdrop: The divine council courtroom scene in 22:31-32 has direct parallels in the book of Job (1:6-12; 2:1-6), where Satan appears before the divine council and demands permission to test Job. The same legal framework operates: the accuser (satan) petitions the divine court; the petition is granted within limits; the faithful one is tested. Zechariah 3:1-5 provides another parallel: Satan stands before the angel of YHWH to accuse Joshua the high priest; YHWH rebukes Satan and restores Joshua. Jesus' phrase 'this is your hour and the power of darkness' (22:53) acknowledges that the hostile powers have been granted a temporary window of authority -- the 'opportune time' Satan waited for since 4:13.

second_temple.summary: Prince Mastema (Satan figure) is given authority to test the Israelites during the exodus, including hardening Pharaoh's heart. This is presented as divine council permission: God allows the adversary to operate within defined limits.
second_temple.relevance: Parallels the framework of Luke 22:31-32: Satan receives permission to test Peter, but the Son's intercession ensures ultimate survival.

cross_refs.note: Satan before the divine council demanding permission to test Job -- the direct background to 'Satan demanded to have you' (22:31)
Satan accusing Joshua the high priest before the angel of YHWH -- another divine council courtroom scene
'Into your hand I commit my spirit' -- Jesus' last words from the cross (23:46); a psalm of trust in extremis
'He was numbered with the transgressors' -- quoted by Jesus in 22:37; fulfilled in his crucifixion between two criminals
Mark's parallel passion: more compressed, without the Herod trial, the angel in Gethsemane, or the penitent thief dialogue
'He always lives to make intercession for them' -- the ongoing ministry that begins with 'I have prayed for you' (22:32)

luke_jerusalem.divine_council_note: Luke 22 contains the most explicit divine council courtroom scene in the Gospels. Satan's 'return' (22:3, 'Satan entered into Judas'; cf. 4:13, 'he departed until an opportune time') signals that the final battle has begun. The demand to 'sift' the disciples (22:31) uses exetesato -- formal legal language for a petition before an authority. Satan appears before the divine court (as in Job 1-2) and demands the right to test Peter and the others. The request is granted -- the sifting will happen -- but Jesus has 'prayed for you that your faith may not fail' (22:32). The Son's intercession before the Father protects the disciples from destruction even while permitting the test. This is the divine council dynamic: testing is real, the adversary is active, but the Son's advocacy ensures the faithful survive. Jesus' statement 'this is your hour, and the power (exousia) of darkness' (22:53) acknowledges that the hostile powers have a brief, authorized window of operation. The darkness (skotos) is not merely nightfall but the domain of the enemy -- the spiritual 'power of darkness' that rules the present age (cf. Col 1:13, 'He has delivered us from the domain of darkness'). The angel strengthening Jesus in Gethsemane (22:43) is a divine council member ministering to the Son at his hour of greatest need -- echoing the angels who ministered after the temptation (Matt 4:11). From the cross, Jesus forgives ('Father, forgive them,' 23:34), promises paradise to the penitent thief (23:43), and commits his spirit to the Father (23:46). Even in the 'hour of darkness,' the Son exercises divine authority: forgiving, saving, and trusting.

sections.heading: Satan Returns: The Last Supper and Gethsemane (22:1-53)
sections.body: 'Then Satan entered into Judas called Iscariot' (22:3) -- the adversary who departed in 4:13 returns at the 'opportune time.' Judas agrees to betray Jesus (22:4-6). At the Passover meal, Jesus says: 'I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer' (22:15). He institutes the Lord's Supper: 'This is my body, which is given for you... This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood' (22:19-20). Luke's unique addition: 'the new covenant (kaine diatheke)' -- explicitly invoking Jeremiah 31:31-34. A dispute about greatness arises (22:24-27); Jesus teaches: 'I am among you as the one who serves' (22:27). Then the divine council warning: 'Simon, Simon, behold, Satan demanded (exetesato) to have you (plural: hymas), that he might sift you like wheat. But I have prayed for you (singular: sou) that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned again, strengthen your brothers' (22:31-32). Peter protests; Jesus predicts the triple denial. In Gethsemane, Jesus prays: 'Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done' (22:42). An angel from heaven strengthens him (22:43). His sweat becomes 'like great drops of blood' (22:44). Judas arrives with a kiss. Jesus heals the servant's ear (unique to Luke). 'This is your hour, and the power (exousia) of darkness' (22:53).

sections.heading: Trial, Crucifixion, and Death (22:54 - 23:56)
sections.body: Peter denies Jesus three times; 'the Lord turned and looked at Peter' (22:61, unique to Luke) -- a devastating silent glance. Peter weeps bitterly (22:62). Before the Sanhedrin: 'If you are the Christ, tell us.' Jesus: 'If I tell you, you will not believe... From now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God' (22:67-69). Before Pilate (23:1-5). Before Herod Antipas (23:6-12, unique to Luke) -- Herod hopes for a miracle, mocks Jesus, and sends him back. Pilate declares Jesus innocent three times (23:4, 14, 22) but yields to the crowd. On the way to Golgotha, Jesus tells the daughters of Jerusalem: 'Do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children' (23:28) -- prophetic warning of 70 AD. On the cross: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (23:34). Two criminals are crucified with him; one mocks, one repents. 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom' (23:42). 'Truly, I say to you, today you will be with me in paradise (paradeiso)' (23:43). Darkness covers the land from the sixth to the ninth hour (23:44). The Temple curtain tears (23:45). 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!' (23:46, Ps 31:5). He breathes his last. The centurion: 'Certainly this man was innocent (dikaios -- righteous)!' (23:47). Joseph of Arimathea buries Jesus (23:50-56).

luke_jerusalem.title: The Resurrection, the Emmaus Road, and the Comprehensive Fulfillment
luke_jerusalem.synopsis: Luke 24 is one of the most theologically rich chapters in the Bible. Women discover the empty tomb; two angels declare 'He is not here, but has risen' (24:6). Peter runs to the tomb and finds it empty (24:12). Two disciples walking to Emmaus are joined by the risen Jesus, unrecognized, who explains 'in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself' (24:27). He is recognized in the breaking of bread and vanishes (24:31). They return to Jerusalem where Jesus appears to the gathered disciples, shows his wounds, eats fish, and delivers the comprehensive fulfillment statement: 'Everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled' (24:44). He opens their minds to understand the Scriptures (24:45) and commissions them: 'repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations (panta ta ethne), beginning from Jerusalem' (24:47). He promises 'the promise of my Father' -- the Holy Spirit (24:49) -- and ascends from Bethany (24:50-51).

key_verse.text: Then he said to them, 'These are my words that I spoke to you while I was still with you, that everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled.' Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures, and said to them, 'Thus it is written, that the Christ should suffer and on the third day rise from the dead, and that repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem.'

original_terms: nomos Mouseos, prophetai, psalmoi (Law of Moses, Prophets, Psalms -- the three divisions of the Hebrew Bible (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim); Jesus claims them all as christological)
dianoigo (to open -- 'he opened their minds (dianoixen ton noun) to understand the Scriptures' (24:45); a divine act of illumination; the same verb used for the opening of eyes at Emmaus (24:31))
panta ta ethne (all nations -- the scope of the commission; the Deuteronomy 32 nations being reclaimed; the Babel reversal proclaimed)
epangelia tou patros (the promise of the Father -- the Holy Spirit; the power from on high that will enable the mission to the nations; fulfilled at Pentecost, Acts 2)
klasis tou artou (the breaking of bread -- Jesus recognized at Emmaus in this act (24:30-31, 35); the eucharistic meal as the context of resurrection recognition)
luke_jerusalem.ane_backdrop: The Emmaus road narrative follows the pattern of divine encounter stories: a deity or divine messenger appears in disguise, travels with humans, reveals truth, and then vanishes. The Genesis 18 theophany (three visitors to Abraham, one revealed as YHWH) and the Tobit story (the angel Raphael traveling in disguise) are parallels. The 'opening of the Scriptures' (24:32) echoes the Jewish tradition of Torah interpretation (midrash) -- the risen Christ interprets the entire Hebrew Bible as pointing to himself, a comprehensive hermeneutical claim that reshaped how the early church read the OT.

second_temple.summary: The angel Raphael reveals his true identity after traveling in disguise with Tobias: 'I am Raphael, one of the seven holy angels who present the prayers of the saints.' He then vanishes from their sight.
second_temple.relevance: Parallels the Emmaus pattern: a divine being travels in disguise, reveals himself, and vanishes. Luke's readers familiar with Tobit would recognize the genre.

cross_refs.note: YHWH appears to Abraham in human form -- the theophanic encounter pattern that the Emmaus road narrative echoes
The Suffering Servant and the passion psalm -- among 'the things concerning himself' that Jesus explained from the Scriptures (24:27)
'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption' -- a resurrection text from 'the Psalms' that Jesus claims as fulfilled (24:44)
The nations allotted to the sons of God -- the worldview behind 'repentance proclaimed to all nations' (24:47)
The continuation: Acts opens where Luke ends, with the ascension and the promise of the Spirit
The fulfillment of 'the promise of the Father' (24:49) -- Pentecost, where the Spirit is poured out and the mission to the nations begins

Paul's resurrection creed: 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures... he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures' -- the same 'Scriptures' Jesus opened in Luke 24

luke_jerusalem.divine_council_note: Luke 24:44-47 is the most comprehensive christological hermeneutical statement in the Gospels. Jesus claims that 'everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled' (24:44). The three-part division -- Law, Prophets, Psalms -- matches the Hebrew Bible's structure (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim). The claim is that the entire OT, including the divine council texts in the Psalms (Ps 2, 22, 82, 89, 110), points to and is fulfilled in Jesus. He then 'opened their minds to understand the Scriptures' (24:45) -- a divine act of illumination that enables the disciples to see the christological pattern running through the entire Hebrew Bible. The content of this pattern: 'The Christ should suffer and on the third day rise from the dead, and that repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations (panta ta ethne), beginning from Jerusalem' (24:46-47). This is the Deuteronomy 32 reversal stated as a program: the nations disinherited at Babel will hear the proclamation of repentance and forgiveness 'in his name' -- transferred from the authority of the territorial elohim to the authority of the crucified and risen Son. 'Beginning from Jerusalem' connects the mission to its source: the city where YHWH chose to place his name, the Temple city, the center of the old covenant. From there, the gospel radiates outward to 'all nations.' The 'promise of the Father' (24:49) -- the Holy Spirit, poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2) -- is the power that enables this mission. The ascension (24:50-51) completes the exodos discussed at the Transfiguration (9:31): Jesus' 'departure' through death, resurrection, and ascension is the new exodus that liberates the nations.

sections.heading: The Empty Tomb and the Emmaus Road (24:1-35)
sections.body: On the first day of the week, women come to the tomb with spices. They find the stone rolled away and the body absent (24:2-3). Two men 'in dazzling apparel' (divine council messengers) ask: 'Why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here, but has risen' (24:5-6). They remind the women of Jesus' predictions (24:6-7). The women tell the apostles, who consider it 'an idle tale' (24:11). Peter runs to the tomb and finds only linen cloths (24:12). That same day, two disciples walk to Emmaus (about 7 miles from Jerusalem). Jesus joins them, unrecognized. They tell him about the crucifixion and their dashed hopes: 'We had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel' (24:21). Jesus responds: 'O foolish ones, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Was it not necessary (edei -- divine necessity) that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?' (24:25-26). 'And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself' (24:27). At Emmaus, he takes bread, blesses and breaks it, and gives it to them. 'Their eyes were opened, and they recognized him. And he vanished from their sight' (24:31). They say: 'Did not our hearts burn within us while he talked to us on the road, while he opened to us the Scriptures?' (24:32).

sections.heading: The Appearance, the Commission, and the Ascension (24:36-53)
sections.body: The two rush back to Jerusalem and find the Eleven gathered. 'The Lord has risen indeed, and has appeared to Simon!' (24:34). As they speak, Jesus himself stands among them: 'Peace to you!' (24:36). They are 'startled and frightened and thought they saw a spirit (pneuma)' (24:37). Jesus shows his hands and feet: 'Touch me, and see. For a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see that I have' (24:39). He eats broiled fish before them (24:42-43) -- demonstrating bodily resurrection. Then the comprehensive statement: 'Everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled' (24:44). 'He opened their minds to understand the Scriptures' (24:45). The commission: 'repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations (panta ta ethne), beginning from Jerusalem' (24:47). 'You are witnesses of these things' (24:48). 'Behold, I am sending the promise of my Father upon you. But stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high' (24:49). At Bethany, 'he lifted up his hands and blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven' (24:50-51). They return to Jerusalem 'with great joy' and are 'continually in the temple blessing God' (24:52-53). The Gospel that began in the Temple ends in the Temple. Volume 2 (Acts) will begin where Volume 1 leaves off.

luke_cosmic_war.title: The Temptation — Three Rebellions Echoed, One Son Unmoved
luke_cosmic_war.synopsis: Luke's Temptation narrative is not a morality story about resisting personal sin. It is a cosmic confrontation between the Last Adam and the adversary who has held the nations in his grip since Babel. [A] Satan explicitly claims authority over all the kingdoms of the world: 'it has been delivered to me' (4:6, emoi paradedotai) — passive voice implying the Father himself allotted this authority at the Babel disinheritance of Deuteronomy 32:8-9. [A] Each of the three temptations structurally echoes one of the Three Rebellions: the bread-from-stones temptation echoes Eden (provision outside God's mandate), the kingdoms temptation echoes Babel (territorial authority over the nations), and the Temple pinnacle temptation echoes the Watchers (twisting divine speech to leverage heavenly power for earthly advantage). [B] Jesus defeats all three by quoting Deuteronomy — the very covenantal document that describes the Babel disinheritance.
